data.title: Energy Stocks And The XLE: Outlook For 2026
data.description:
As markets enter 2026, traditional energy will be a critical economic factor, as oil and gas continue to power the United States and the world.

Crude oil prices have declined in 2025, while natural gas prices have increased. The State Street® Energy Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLE) holds shares of the leading U.S. integrated oil and gas companies and other traditional energy-related companies. Meanwhile, the XLE has outperformed crude oil prices in 2025, and I expect earnings for the traditional energy sector to remain robust in 2026, thereby rating XLE a Hold given its attractive dividend yield in a falling interest rate environment.

Crude oil prices decline in 2025- Natural gas prices rally

Since the end of 2024, NYMEX and Brent crude oil futures prices have experienced double-digit percentage declines.

The monthly continuous chart of NYMEX WTI crude oil futures highlights the 20% decline from $71.72 at the end of 2024 to $57.32 per barrel on December 11, 2025.

The monthly continuous chart of ICE Brent crude oil futures highlights the 18.3% decline from $74.64 at the end of 2024 to $60.95 per barrel on December 11, 2025.

The monthly continuous chart of NYMEX U.S. natural gas futures highlights the 36.6% rise from $3.098 at the end of 2024 to $4.231 per MMBtu on December 11, 2025.

Meanwhile, U.K. natural gas futures prices have declined in 2025.

The monthly continuous chart of ICE U.K. natural gas futures highlights the 42.2% decline from $121.96 at the end of 2024 to $70.52 per 1,000 therms on December 11, 2025.

Dutch Title Transfer Facility (TTF) gas futures are a virtual trading point and the leading marketplace for natural gas in Europe, and have also declined since the end of 2024.

The monthly continuous chart of Dutch TTF natural gas futures highlights the 45.2% decline from 48.889 at the end of 2024 to 26.805 on December 11, 2025.

While worldwide crude oil and European natural gas prices have declined significantly, U.S. natural gas prices have rallied in 2025.

Energy stocks rally- The XLE posts gains and experiences a stock split

While the overall picture for energy commodities has been bearish in 2025, energy companies have profited, and their shares have rallied with the overall stock market. The State Street Energy Select SPDR ETF, the most liquid traditional energy ETF, has moved higher in 2025.

The chart highlights XLE’s 7.8% gain from $42.83 at the end of 2024 to $46.18 per share on December 11, 2025. At $46.18 per share, XLE had over $27.5 billion in assets under management. XLE trades an average of over 29.7 million shares daily and charges a nominal 0.08% management fee. The blended $1.44 dividend equates to a 3.12% yield. XLE experienced a 2:1 stock split on December 5, 2025.

The XLE has an over 40% exposure to the two leading U.S. traditional integrated energy companies

The top holdings of the XLE ETF include:

The chart shows that XLE’s top 10 holdings include a 40.26% exposure to Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX), the two leading U.S. integrated energy companies. XLE also owns other leading oil and gas companies, as well as energy-related processing and refining, equipment, and services companies. Meanwhile, oil services have done even better than the XLE in 2025.

The VanEck Oil Services ETF (OIH) owns shares in the leading services companies, including an over 36% exposure to Schlumberger (SLB), Baker Hughes (BKR), and Halliburton (HAL).

The chart shows the 14.3% rise in the OIH ETF from $271.23 at the end of 2024 to $309.96 per share on December 11. OIH has outperformed XLE in 2025.

Crude oil prices can continue to fall, and earnings for XLE holdings can increase for three compelling reasons

Crude oil prices (CL1:COM, CO1:COM) remain in a bearish trend in late 2025, with the NYMEX futures not far above the critical technical support at the April 2025 low of $55.12 per barrel. Brent futures are also within striking distance of their technical support at the April 2025 low of $58.39 per barrel. Meanwhile, U.S. natural gas (NG1:COM) has experienced a seasonal rally as the withdrawal season, where inventories decline, started in November, and cold temperatures have increased heating demand.

While crude oil prices continue to trend lower, three factors could continue to support the leading U.S. integrated oil and related energy companies held by the XLE ETF in 2026:

  • The U.S. Trump administration’s energy policy supports production and consumption of oil and gas to reduce inflation, establish U.S. energy independence, and increase U.S. oil and gas exports.
  • The U.S. has been cooperating with OPEC+’s leading member, Saudi Arabia, on many global issues. The Trump administration and the Saudis have likely discussed and agreed on a strategy to manage petroleum prices at levels that encourage production and maintain Saudi and U.S. revenues.
  • While the world continues to move toward alternative energy sources, crude oil and natural gas remain the energy commodities that power the world.

These factors could continue to support crude oil prices and earnings for the leading traditional energy companies in 2026. As we have seen in 2025, declining oil prices have not weighed on the XLE and OIH ETFs.

I rate XLE a hold for 2026- The blended yield and P/E ratio are attractive

The most diversified U.S. stock market index, the S&P 500, and the SPY ETF that tracks the index, have a blended dividend yield of around 1.06%. The SPY’s blended P/E ratio is currently around the 30 level.

XLE’s blended yield of over 3% and its P/E ratio of around 16.41 times earnings make the ETF attractive. Seeking Alpha’s ETF Grades for XLE are mostly positive.

XLE receives the highest possible A+ grades in the liquidity and expenses categories. The modestly bullish price action earns a C in momentum, while the over 3% yield receives a B in the dividend category. The risk of commodities and the crude oil and natural gas markets earns a D- in the risk category, as energy commodities can experience significant price volatility.

I rate the XLE a hold due to the bearish trend in crude oil prices. The dividend yield and U.S. energy policy will likely drive the ETF to deliver positive returns in the coming year.

data.title: American States Water: Still A Hold Rating After 2 Years
data.description:
In this article, I'll be looking to update my thesis on American States Water Company (AWR), a water company that I last covered over 2 years ago. At the time, I considered the company a Hold and likely to go nowhere due to valuation and a lack of upside. Now, 2 years later (and more), that scenario has very clearly materialized, with the S&P 500 (SP500) up about 60% and the company down almost 5% inclusive of dividends.

My thesis and my rating were therefore correct, insofar as this timeframe went, and I was correct in investing in other businesses instead of AWR.

You can find my latest article on AWR here. This poor performance, of course, begs the question of whether the company is now suddenly a Buy or a positive prospect for a conservative investment.

My answer to that would be twofold. First off, we're in a very inflated overall market (my estimate). Secondly, water companies are in need of a correction in valuation to better reflect their risk of capital expenditures, specifically for infrastructure renovations. This is part of the reason why many of these companies have seen declines in their valuations over the past 2 years and why I probably wouldn't forecast any of them above 30x any longer (with some exceptions in some scenarios).

What I want to convey here is that the current market is risk-heavy, and highly valued companies like AWR are definitely part of this risk. On the positive side, the company's once-30x+ P/E is now down to less than 22x average weighted as of the time of writing this article, which, of course, could work as an argument why the company with an A-rating and a decent yield of 2.77% at the time of writing could be a Buy.

So let's see what we have here and what updated estimates tell us here.

American States Water: Lower valuation, still somewhat uncertain upside

Water companies aren't always as stable as we'd like them to be. Part of the reason I was so conservative in the 2023 period was what happened to AWR in 2022. The company made a significant earnings miss at the time, especially towards the end of fiscal '22, due to market volatility. However, at the time regulatory responses from the company's primary regulator, CPUC (in this specific case, the settlement back then did not meet the request for a rate increase or mechanisms that the company wanted to adjust/introduce), had also grown, and thus the company's valuation was in no way “fair” any longer—at least that was how some investors (including me) saw things at the time.

I viewed this as part of a bigger picture as well, where the water utilities across the U.S., depending on their CapEx history, would face increasing headwinds from necessary retrofitting and renovation, coupled with cost and regulatory pressure. In this environment, I saw clear issues in the companies maintaining their premiums.

So, AWR has underperformed significantly. This is despite very strong fundamentals and even very strong EPS growth. Since 2022, the company's EPS has grown over 13-14% on average per year (even if part of that is a reversal from the poor 2022 results). It goes to show you that even when a company performs like this, you can still get very poor returns (negative ones, in this case, even with dividends) if you invest at the wrong valuation.

That's my whole “spiel” as an investor and analyst—I try to find undervalued equities and buy them at that undervaluation. In a high valuation environment, quality and yield will usually not save you—what happened before the dot-com bubble and the GFC is a perfect example. And I believe we're on our way, or are already, into/in the same environment again.

On the positive side, we now have a much clearer and lower valuation for AWR. The key challenge for an investor like me is now finding the new “normalized premium” for these companies, which also takes into consideration what could happen in the event of a market crash or decline (which I believe is coming).

The company's decline of over 20% in a single half-year also dispels quite clearly the notion that water companies cannot be volatile. They certainly can, and are.

Over the years since 2023, many analysts have been positive on the company, for good reasons. The average SA analyst here is at a Buy, while Wall Street and Quant are at a Hold.

In this scenario, I agree more with the latter two than the former. Why is that?

I believe many analysts are underestimating the required premium normalization and are mispricing/not discounting for the potential market downturn we may face, which (while not necessarily involving AWR), will drag down AWR as well, if the 40-year history of crashes is any indication.

Furthermore, while I like the company's military base exposure as a good revenue generator, I don't like the company's exposure to California, given the macroeconomic and political trends currently in the U.S., which can be liberally described as “fluid.”

But positives as well—the company's Q3 '25 results were solid. With a variance/growth of almost 10% due to an increase above all in contracted services, this company's mix is a solid one. AWR's business model has more future safety potential than a water company that only focuses on legacy water services. From that lens, there are a lot of positive trends here. Fundamentals are also beyond solid. The company has plenty of cash on hand and is A/A+ rated. If you invest in AWR, you can (in my view) be certain that nothing fundamental is going to happen to make your invested capital disappear.

Though this may not matter if the capital you invest remains unproductive due to declines for years.

Another positive is that recent rate cases with CPUC went far better than in -22. The company got an okay for its capital infrastructure investment, including some capital investments not expected.

However, all is not “gold” here. The CPUC also fully rejected, once again, a decoupling mechanism and a balancing account. Still, the overall result of the decision, including the decision for Sutter Pointe, can be characterized as “hard but fair.” Given the operating geography of California, it's to be expected.

It also should be mentioned that this company remains a clear dividend king, with consecutive raises of 71 years and counting. This makes it one of the most solid dividend kings on the entire market, with dividends paid since the tail end of the financial depression of the 1930s.

The challenge for the company is neither earnings nor fundamentals, as these are very solid. Instead, the challenge is where we should be valuing the company on both a current and forward basis—and different analysts have different answers to that question.

Valuation for AWR: There's upside, but it's risky

Different answers.

If we look at the 20-year P/E average, which includes almost 8 years of 27x+ P/E valuations, the average comes to 26.78x, or, on a forward basis, a fair value of $105/share, which, from $72/share, would see us having an upside of 20% per year. From that forecast, the prospective PT for the company would be $80/share, and the company would now be a Buy.

That's what I believe to be the bullish thesis for this company,

My argument against this 26-27x P/E valuation is based on two things.

First, the company operates in higher-risk geographies with more challenging regulatory trends. A 27-30x P/E valuation might be something I would accept in a safer environment, but this is not that. It's also a fact that the 26-27x P/E average is discolored somewhat by ZIRP. If we were to remove those years from the equation, the average P/E valuation would very quickly drop to 22-23x P/E, the valuation the company had during and after the GFC. During the long years of 2010 and 2011, the company also traded close to 15x P/E. You might think this to be unrealistic or unheard of for a water business, but this was, in fact, where the company was at the time.

Secondly, the current market valuation and inflation thereof. It's clear to me (and others) that the market in its current state is much higher than it has any right to be. We're not in dot-com territory, but we're approaching it. I have gone to over 40% cash, and while I still invest, I maintain shorter, more agile, and smaller investments than I typically do to preserve cash. This is more important to me than eking out gains in this market, because when the decline comes, I firmly believe it will be significant.

In my last article, I gave the company a PT of $77/share. It's currently at $72/share. This would make the company a Buy. But the risk/reward ratio at the time in 2023 was different, so I'm updating it.

I believe the company should trade at 23-24x P/E, which implies a PT of $88/share for 2027E at current estimates, which have a high likelihood of accuracy. Based on $88/share, I'm lowering my PT to $71/share for a 15% upside, as it's currently closer to 13% per year.

Even with the price at $71/share, I would carefully consider whether this company would be a good choice. There are water company alternatives with more constructive rate environments, better yields, and similar fundamentals. York Water (YORW) has recovered somewhat from the price I purchased it at, but is still at a 15% annualized upside—I consider a 26-27x P/E valid here. Even American Water Works (AWK), a company I cover, can be considered a Buy at a 24-25x P/E forecast range—they are significantly larger than AWR as well, with better earnings accuracy.

All in all, I consider AWR to be the weaker prospect here and give it a Hold. Risks to the company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

There are things to like about AWR here, but unfortunately, the company's upside isn't one of them. I believe many of my colleagues are mistaking a “lower valuation” for a “low valuation” in context, and I believe you should really be discounting it more due to market trends. Aside from being overvalued on certain metrics like sales and underperforming sector averages here, the main risks are related to things that AWR does not control. We're talking about challenging regulations, environmental factors, inflation, operating costs, and insurance costs (in California, these are massive due to wildfires, etc.).

Because of this, I do not view the company as properly discounted relative to risk, and other companies are more attractive here.

My updated thesis is as follows.

Thesis

AWR is a dividend king and a water company with an A rating and good levels of overall safety. It has an attractive operating structure and mixes around 80% regulated business with around 20% unregulated. The company has degrees of future-proofing and is a conservative allocator of capital, largely backed by the state of California, which, in my view, comes with a bit of a risk.

At the right and attractive price, this company becomes, as I see it, a “must buy”—but that time is not today. Despite better valuation, I'm lowering my price target (“PT”) and fair value here.

I view AWR as a hold, and I wouldn't touch it above a PT of $71/share. Even $71/share is high, but at least there I could see a conservative upside, which I really can't see at this price, updated for the Q3 '25 period.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative, and well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 “stars” here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $71/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a Hold rating as of December 2025.

data.title: Are You Prepared For A Bear Market? Best Stocks To Target Now
data.description:
Registration is now open for our Top Stocks for 2026 event with Steven Cress, featuring his newest list of high-conviction picks designed to target long-term performance. Steve’s previous Top Stocks lists have demonstrated an impressive track record, so don’t miss out on this much-anticipated event.

Best Stocks to Buy in the Next Correction or Bear Market

Market corrections and bear markets may be unsettling, but they consistently present some of the best opportunities to build long-term wealth. History shows that equities spend far more time in bull markets than in drawdowns. Additionally, exiting early and waiting for the perfect re-entry point almost guarantees missing the biggest upside, and the cost of missing just a handful of the biggest up days can be severe. This summarizes the buy-and-hold investing mantra: time in the market beats timing the market.

According to a study on the 30 years of stock market performance from 1995 through 2024, 78% of the market’s best days have occurred during a bear market or during the first two months of a bull market. If you missed the market’s 10 best days in that time frame, your returns would have been cut in half. If you missed the best 30 days, you would have reduced your returns by 83%

The counterargument is that missing the worst days can produce similar results as catching the best days. However, a counterargument to this is that buying shares of beaten-down, high-quality stocks during the steepest corrections can potentially yield better results than the above. To paraphrase Warren Buffett, you can make the most money in bear markets by buying great companies at discounted prices. My article illustrates this philosophy in action: make a target list of “great companies” now and be prepared to buy shares during the next severe correction or a bear market.

How I Selected the Best Stocks to Buy in the Next Correction

To choose the best stocks to buy in the next correction or bear market, I used the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener and filtered for Quant Strong Buys, Buys and Holds and added the metrics for Sector & Industry and P/E forward. I sought out stocks with high valuations and targeted companies with strengths in the other key factors of Growth, Profitability, Momentum, and Revisions. The intention is to isolate companies that can grow more attractive at lower price points.

1. Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD)

  • Market Capitalization: $360.48B
  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy
  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/11/2025): 23 out of 536
  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/11/2025): 6 out of 67
  • Sector: Information Technology
  • Industry: Semiconductors

Advanced Micro Devices is a leading semiconductor company designing high-performance CPUs, GPUs, and AI accelerators used across data centers, PCs, gaming devices, and embedded systems. Mentioned this week by President Trump as a company that may get approved sales of GPUs to China, AMD sits at the center of multiple secular growth drivers: the global buildout of AI infrastructure, rising data-center compute needs, and continued share gains in both client and server processors.

While AMD’s 55.84 P/E is more than double the sector median’s 25.12, my preferred valuation metric for a growth stock, the PEG ratio, is a reasonable 1.33, which is nearly 25% below the sector median. This suggests that AMD is relatively well priced compared to its growth prospects. AMD receives an ‘A’ for its growth factor grade, which is supported by a solid 23.83% forward EBITDA, which is nearly double the sector median. I also like AMD’s strength in free cash flow per share forward growth rate of over 85%, indicating ample cash for operations.

A broad market correction or semiconductor pullback typically compresses AMD’s multiples, creating more attractive entry points. For long-term investors, companies like AMD represent a durable compounder with multi-year upside potential tied to the AI cycle. This idea transitions well into our next target in our list, another semiconductor company.

2. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.23T
  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy
  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/11/2025): 7 out of 538
  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/11/2025): 3 out of 67
  • Sector: Information Technology
  • Industry: Semiconductors

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) is a leading pure-play semiconductor foundry, producing advanced chips for blue-chip customers, such as Apple (AAPL), NVDA, and AMD. Its scale, manufacturing knowledge, and EUV (Extreme Ultraviolet Lithography) leadership create an impressive moat, as it sits at the center of secular AI trends, high-performance computing, and 5G. That quality and strategic importance often command a premium valuation that may be a bit lofty for some investors, while attractive to others, especially after looking at its growth potential.

TSM’s 29.77 P/E is approximately 19% above the sector median, but its 1.18 PEG is a comfortable 33% below the sector, so aggressive investors may like this stock now, while others may target TSM as a buy during the next correction or bear market. Turning to its growth metrics, TSM shines as it earns an 'A' in that factor. Further supporting its growth story, TSM’s November sales jump of 24.5% year-over-year may help the semiconductor foundry giant top analyst expectations, Wedbush Securities said.

For long-term investors, broad market or chip sector corrections are often the best opportunities to accumulate stocks like TSM at more reasonable valuations. This brings us to another potential AI winner, especially at lower price levels, but this time in the communications sector.

3. Ciena Corporation (CIEN)

  • Market Capitalization: $31.29B
  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy
  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/11/2025): 16 out of 536
  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/11/2025): 4 out of 38
  • Sector: Information Technology
  • Industry: Semiconductors

Ciena is a global leader in optical networking systems, software, and services that power the high capacity backbones of telecom networks, cloud providers, and hyperscale data centers. As AI workloads surge, demand for ultra-high-bandwidth connectivity and more efficient optical transport has accelerated, positioning CIEN squarely in the path of long-term infrastructure growth. While other key fundamentals remain strong, the stock carries a valuation premium.

With a forward P/E of 88.72, which is more than triple the sector median, I immediately turn to the PEG for validation. In this case, it’s a bit high at 1.88, although it’s near the sector median, hence its 'C+' relative grade for this growth-central metric. This underscores the importance of seeing a lower price point for entry for investors considering CIEN as an addition to their watchlist. Keep in mind that CIEN still receives a Quant Strong Buy, which is due to the company’s high growth and momentum scores. Highlighting this point is CIEN’s Q4 results reported Dec. 11, the morning this article was published. CIEN’s price jumped 5% in early trading as the networking systems provider blew past earnings estimates. CIEN shares jumped more than 5% on Thursday after the networking systems provider reported better-than-expected results and issued strong guidance for the coming quarter and fiscal year. "Looking ahead, we are confident in our growth trajectory over the coming years, driven by durable demand from our cloud and service provider customers and a growing set of opportunities inside and around the data center," said Ciena president and CEO, Gary Smith, in a statement.

For long-term investors, broad market pullbacks or telecom-specific slowdowns often create entry opportunities into a critical player with great potential to benefit from rising data traffic and AI-driven network upgrades. For our next growth stock target for buying in the next correction or bear market, we shift out of pure play AI stocks into the industrials sector.

4. Power Solutions International, Inc. (PSIX)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.46B
  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy
  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/11/2025): 32 out of 617
  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/11/2025): 3 out of 13
  • Sector: Industrials
  • Industry: Heavy Electrical Equipment

Power Solutions International designs and manufactures advanced, emission-certified engines and power systems for industrial, transportation, and energy applications with a focus on non-diesel fuels and cleaner power solutions. The company benefits from tightening emission standards and the ongoing replacement of legacy engine platforms. While PSIX dropped 16% last month after it reported weak industrial and transportation demand, and it faced margin pressure, its long-term growth prospects remain promising. For example, its gross profit margin was 27.17%, which is a bit below the sector median of 12.13. But digging into its attractive trailing PEG of 0.10, coupled with its forward earnings potential, there’s rationale to add PSIX to a buy watchlist.

PSIX earns an 'A+' growth grade for a number of factors, including its sector-crushing forward EBITDA growth of over 44% and forward EPS expansion expected at more than 70%, compared to less than 10% for sector peers. With strong forward earnings growth potential, yet volatile short-term performance tendencies, PSIX carries small-cap, high-beta characteristics. For investors willing to ride that volatility, the next market pullback that drags PSIX down with it can offer an attractive entry into a niche industrial growth business with improving profitability.

Turning from the high-growth semiconductor and industrial stocks, we shift to the communications sector, another growth area that has been gaining attention recently.

5. Deutsche Telekom AG (DTEGY)

  • Market Capitalization: $149.39B
  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy
  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/11/2025): 2 out of 229
  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/11/2025): 2 out of 25
  • Sector: Communication Services
  • Industry: Integrated Telecommunication Services

Deutsche Telecom is one of Europe’s largest telecommunications operators and the majority owner of T-Mobile US, combining relatively stable European telecom cash flows with higher-growth U.S. wireless exposure. The communications sector is in the spotlight now as the bidding war for Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) between Paramount Skydance (PSKY) and Netflix (NFLX) looks to stretch into 2026. Meanwhile, DTEGY generates substantial EBITDA, offers a solid yield of 3.16%, yet still trades at a modest earnings and EV/EBITDA multiple versus its sector peers.

Compared to the other four stocks on our list, DTEGY already trades at a decent P/E at 12.88, which is comfortably below the sector median at 16.01. Although it's not exactly a growth stock by definition, I like seeing the low PEG of 1.33, which is 8.5% below DTEGY’s sector peers. Turning to the growth numbers, the global telecom company’s earnings growth potential looks solid for the coming year, with forward EBITDA above 12%, which is more than double the sector median.

While DTEGY is less "growthy" than the other names on this list of stocks to buy in a correction or bear market, it has news cycle sensitivity (falling in price alongside NFLX while the S&P 500 remained mostly positive), which has widened its value gap. For long-term investors seeking a blend of income, defensive characteristics, and U.S. wireless participation, those dislocations can be attractive entry points, albeit for slightly different reasons than the other stocks in my list.

Conclusion: Best Stocks to Buy in the Next Correction or Bear Market

Market corrections and bear markets can be uncomfortable, but they remain some of the most reliable entry points for long-term investors. Because equities spend far more time advancing than declining, the odds of perfectly timing the bottom are extremely low, and missing even a few strong rebound days can materially weaken long-term returns. A more effective approach is to stay primarily invested while maintaining a disciplined watchlist of high-quality names. AMD, TSM, CIEN, PSIX, and DTEGY each have compelling multi-year growth or cash-flow narratives, but they can trade at elevated valuations during strong market phases. Using corrections to accumulate shares of companies like these reduces valuation risk while maintaining exposure to powerful secular themes, such as AI compute, semiconductor demand, data transport upgrades, industrial modernization, and global telecom growth. The strategy is not about predicting downturns; it’s about being prepared to capitalize on them.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top "strong buy" quant stocks. Seeking Alpha’s quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

If you’re looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It’s a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps. Happy investing!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dell: Undervalued So Long As The AI Push Remains In Place
data.description:
Analysts and investors can rightly debate the real-world impact that AI is having on businesses in general, but there’s little debate that the AI capex cycle has been transformative for Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL). Two and a half years ago, the story about Dell largely revolved around how the company was going to navigate the fundamentally mediocre growth prospects for its legacy PC and server hardware business. Now the story is largely centered on whether AI-driven data center capex spending can sustain high-single-digit revenue growth across the next five years and beyond.

Dell shares have more than tripled since my last Seeking Alpha article rating it a "Buy," handily outperforming rivals like HP Inc. (HPQ), Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (HPE), and Lenovo Group Limited (LNVGY), though not matching the runaway performance of silicon providers like Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA).

Unlike a lot of AI-driven stories, Dell’s valuation looks surprisingly moderate to me; so much so that it leads me to question my modeling assumptions. Clearly, any bull thesis on these shares rests on ongoing double-digit growth in data center capex spending, but all things considered, Dell comes off looking like a relative bargain option for playing this well-loved trend, even with threats and pressures like competition, memory chip prices, and the risk of revisions to data center capex budgets.

The Spike In Memory Will Bite, But It Should Be Manageable

As I discussed in a recent article on Lenovo, soaring memory prices are threatening the margins of major PC and server hardware players like Dell. Between increased demand from AI applications and underinvestment from memory chip manufacturers, DRAM prices have jumped about 4x since late September, while NAND prices have risen around 60% year to date.

Surging memory prices hit various product lines differently. Not surprisingly, memory chips are a huge part of the bill of materials for all-flash array storage systems (over 70% in many cases), and they’re a surprisingly large portion of more traditional servers (35% to 55%). For PCs, the ratio is closer to 15%-25% (higher for AI-enabled PCs), but for AI servers, it's less than 10% (largely because other components are so much more expensive).

There’s also likely to be substantial company-by-company variance. Companies like Dell and Lenovo have top-tier sourcing capabilities (and the ability to make component suppliers “share the pain” to some extent), as well as the capital flexibility to build up inventory – something that both companies have been doing. Beyond that, they will have to raise prices in some cases, and Dell has already announced price hikes for PC customers.

All things considered, I think Dell is well-positioned here compared to its rivals. Companies don’t report in ways that make for easy apples-to-apples comparisons, but Dell’s PC business is less than half of total revenue now, while Lenovo’s IDG business (which also includes tablets and smartphones) is 75% of revenue, and HP is about 70% leveraged to PCs. This is where I expect the most demand destruction from higher prices and where margins don’t really allow companies to not raise prices to offset these pressures (after accounting for other mitigation strategies).

On the server side, AI-optimized servers are about half of Dell’s server business (and 20% of overall revenue); Lenovo doesn’t offer as much information, but looking at third-party reports, I’d estimate it’s closer to 10% of total revenue.

One aspect of all of this that needs to be watched is the extent to which Dell chooses to absorb margin hits to protect certain businesses. Dell has been prioritizing its premium commercial PC business over the last couple of years, and this tends to be a less price-sensitive market, giving the company more flexibility. Likewise, although memory isn’t a meaningful cost for AI servers, it’s also a less price-sensitive market where the company could possibly recoup some margin. On the flip side, I expect management will be less willing to eat bad margins on consumer PCs (especially outside the premium segment) and will cede share if need be.

Sustainability Is The Big Unknown Now

I don’t find much to fault in Dell’s recent financial performance. Fiscal third quarter results saw 11% overall revenue growth, with 24% growth in the Infrastructure Solutions business, driven by over 90% growth in AI-optimized servers. Operating margin improved 10 bp yoy to 9.3%, though with some erosion in the ISG segment (down 90 bp to 12.4%).

The biggest question is whether the AI-driven data center capex gravy train will continue to roll on, because without it, growth will be quite a bit less impressive. The Commercial Solutions business (which is largely PCs, with some peripherals as well) grew 3% this quarter, below the 12% growth in Lenovo’s comparable IDG business, and the traditional server business was only up about 1%, while storage was down 1%.

In the short term, the business still looks fine. Major hyperscalers like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) have given no indication that they’re about to ease off, and large enterprises are committing to their own AI-enabling data center buildouts as well.

To that end, Dell saw AI orders up 242%, with a pipeline that management described as “multiples” above its $18.4B backlog (up over 300%). Likewise, looking at the orders and backlogs from infrastructure-related companies like Comfort Systems USA, Inc. (FIX), Eaton Corporation plc (ETN), and Vertiv Holdings Co (VRT) lends credence to the idea that capex spending will continue to increase at a double-digit rate at least through 2027.

Still, trees don’t grow to the sky. As has been well-discussed elsewhere, ChatGPT is spending many multiples of revenue on capex, and while that’s a single example, it’s alarming to consider the stat that Dell offered in its October Analyst Day – 45% of GDP is currently being driven by AI capex. If investors start getting nervous about the returns on the capital being funneled into capex (as happened with EVs, though on a much smaller scale), that’s going to be an issue. Likewise, there are questions about whether it’s physically possible to install the sort of power generation and transmission infrastructure needed to support the more bullish predictions for AI hardware growth over the next decade-plus.

The Outlook

While the biggest (or perhaps “loudest”) AI bulls are almost certainly overstating the utility of AI in the near term (as anybody who uses ChatGPT could likely confirm), the sheer number of demonstrated use cases does support the investment case to some extent. The current pace of investment isn’t sustainable, but Dell can still do well when capex growth inevitably slows.

I’m looking for around 8% annualized revenue growth over the next five years (in line with management’s recently raised target of 7% to 9%) and long-term growth of around 5% to 6%. I’m not looking for that much EBITDA margin leverage in the near term, as pressure from memory prices and stiff competition across all lines of business is likely to keep EBITDA margins around 11% and free cash flow margins around 6% to 7%. I see some upside to over 7% over time, though, and that’s all still enough to drive FCF growth modestly above revenue growth over the longer term.

Between discounted cash flow and margin-driven multiples, I think Dell is undervalued today below $165 to $170, with DCF supporting the lower end and a 10x forward multiple on EBITDA supporting the higher end (driven, in turn, by operating margins in the low 8% range). I’m not really a fan of P/E-based metrics for companies like Dell, but I would note the shares trade at less than 12x the current average sell-side estimate for FY’27, and I think 15%+ EPS growth over the next three to five years is a reasonable estimate.

The Bottom Line

Given my concerns that there’s so little room left for AI-driven data center capex spending to impress to the good, I’m surprised that I end up still liking the valuation at Dell. As a “picks and shovels” provider of essential hardware, I like the basic investment thesis here, and I think the valuation is reasonable enough that investors who still want to try to generate alpha from AI can look at this name as a credible candidate.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: IQVIA: Increasingly Indispensable In Drug Development
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The scale of IQVIA Holdings (IQV) in healthcare information, real-world evidence collection, and AI-enabled trials and commercialization make it much more than a large CRO. Other CROs compete for share within the contract research segment, but IQVIA is expanding the category itself, making itself an indispensable backbone of the life-sciences industry.

Apart from that distinction, IQVIA has other strong points. Its revenue visibility is long term and backlog driven. Its margin expansion is backed by at least four kinds of technology innovations - Decentralized trials, Secondary control arms, Digital platforms, and Clinical and commercial SaaS applications. Finally, its EPS growth follows from large scale structured buybacks (over $1 billion in the first 9 months of 2025).

As we see from company commentary, this compounding is visible in Q3 free cash flow, which was the highest in the company’s history, even outpacing the covid era boom. Backlog climbed to a record $32.4 billion with a 1.15x net book-to-bill ratio. Together, these drivers support a sustainable compounder: contracted revenue + strong margins + buyback-enhanced EPS. That combination is rare in the life-sciences services space.

The Embedded Infra of Drug Development

IQVIA does much more than clinical trial support. It maintains more than 1.2 billion non-identified patient records, stores over 120 billion healthcare records every year from over 150,000 data providers. This multi-modal dataset is used in clinical development in various ways. It helps analyse trial feasibility and protocol design. IQVIA’s data is used for site selection. Patient data is used for enrollment. Data is needed for real-world evidence, an increasingly critical component of post-approval drugs. Finally, provider, prescription and insurance data is used for commercialisation.

This type of data goes way beyond what a standard CRO normally does. This level of global, granular, sophisticated intelligence sourced from past trials, hospitals, prescribers, HCPs and insurance providers underpins much of the decision-making process in the global life sciences industry. Currently, IQVIA is, if not the sole, but the most comprehensive provider of this data.

We saw this sentiment reflected in the earnings call, where management told us that “new drug launches continue to be a key area of strength.” There were many instances where clients purchased not just trial execution but full-suite data assets and analytics. This is ample proof that commercialization decisions are increasingly driven by IQVIA’s data.

IQVIA’s competitive positioning in data intelligence makes it difficult for clients to switch. Firstly, almost no one else offers this level of data. Second, even if they did, there is considerable cost involved in switching between one dataset to another. Essentially, this helps IQVIA get a captured market - and those long-term contracts. It does not matter which therapeutic cycle is prominent at any point of time. Be it oncology, neurology or obesity, all segments need data - data that only IQVIA can provide at scale.

I look at this data like an infrastructure asset. It needs one time capital expense to generate the pipeline for the data. Once it is there, it keeps compounding as the drug development business grows, clinical trial volume increases, trial designs become more complex, and regulators become increasingly demanding of real world outcomes data. IQVIA can use the same database again and again to increase earnings. This makes its revenue much more visible. IQVIA does not need to generate new clients every quarter. All it needs to do is to convert backlog into current income. It is insulated from customer churn, because even if drug sponsors reduce individual programs, switching a provider like IQVIA becomes next to impossible.

As management noted in the Q3 call, this insulation shows up in reduced cancellations from the IRA-related reprioritizations that occurred during 2023. Large pharma RFP has grown 20% year over year. This shows that there is accelerated growth in IQVIA’s data dependent revenue model.

A Defensible AI Moat

The advent of AI has created a stir in the healthcare/CRO/trials ecosystem - as if AI is going to solve all problems. That’s not how it works, though. Without properly aggregated outcomes-linked data - the kind IQVIA has - AI is rarely actionable. That is why, when IQVIA highlights its Healthcare-grade AI product, it layers it on top of its proprietary datasets.

IQVIA’s clinically contextualized data makes it easier for it to train proprietary AI models. Firstly, this type of tuned AI behaves differently from generic AI. It doesn’t only guess patterns, but it uses relevant data to produce evidence-based inference. Secondly, this regulatory-level data moat cannot be curated quickly. IQVIA’s framework holds privacy, compliance and traceability - key metrics in modern GCx compliant data.

IQVIA, therefore, has little to fear from Big Tech. Big Tech has generic language models, but they simply do not possess the kind of proprietary clinical data IQVIA holds. CROs do not have such data as well - what they have comes from individual trial executions, but IQVIA-type data is comprehensive, not sourced from individual trials alone. It comes from decades of supplier relationships, data rights frameworks and data pipelines, not just scraping - so if Big Tech or CROs or drug sponsors need data, IQVIA is its almost only vendor.

In the earnings call, we see confirmation of this demand: 90 AI “agents” are already in development across 25 use cases. These agents are being used by clients for launches, compliance, marketing and so on - which is much beyond only clinical support.

Evidence and Decentralized Trials Are Margin Tailwinds

Legacy CROs have a labor-scaled model - more trials, more people, more revenue; IQVIA is moving away from that and focusing on margins. They are using technology to replace the most expensive, labor-intensive parts of the trial process. Tools such as decentralized trials and secondary control arms are doing away with the need for expensive placebo groups.

These tools target the exact bottlenecks and cost compounders in the trial process. Shaving every month off enrollment is a huge margin win, because fewer labor hours are tied to program delivery per dollar of revenue. Making a placebo arm redundant is a margin win as well as a scientific win.

These tools are already impacting IQVIA’s topline. As we have seen, Technology & Analytics Solutions grew 6.7% year-to-date, the fastest among IQVIA’s businesses, while field-heavy services grew more slowly. This growth is happening in higher-margin components of the trial process. One other improvement is second-order business. Earlier, or for CRO competitors, services would end at a certain point. Data-driven technology through a decentralized infrastructure creates continuity. This reusable digital platform keeps revenues coming where CRO models had no revenue continuity.

For investors, the takeaway is that IQVIA’s model generates incremental revenue without proportionate labor increase. This is changing the cost structure of clinical development. It is also creating a long term margin tailwind.

Backlog Compounding and Revenue Visibility

IQVIA is not a subscription-based business, but its economics now resembles one. In Q3, their net new bookings rose 13%, and backlog went from $31.1B to $32.4B (+4.1% YoY). This was augmented as ongoing programs were expanded rather than closed.

IQVIA has an impressive list of clients, with nearly all the top 100 global pharma and biotech companies making the list. No single client accounts for 10%+ revenue. All of this helps backlog convert consistently, and spreads the contractual risk across almost the entire industry.

Backlog mix is also geared towards larger companies, those with multiple assets, and spans protocol design to commercialization, as the Q3 call noted. Awards are also moving into full lifecycle services, carrying longer revenue tails than the usual CRO contract.

There is distinct improvement in regulatory schedules, so site activations have improved. Backlog conversion has also improved alongside. That means, revenues come sooner than before, easing working capital needs and boosting operating cash flow.

Management also told us that the drag in emerging biotech spend is offset by big pharma accounts. This stabilized revenue since big pharma programs are stable, are rarely cancelled mid-schedule, and often scale.

Another interesting shift highlighted by management is that IQVIA is now getting called into earlier stages of development. They are creating feasibility and protocol models rather than only coming in at trial execution, something that gives them a comprehensive grip on the trial process. It also increases revenue.

Exposure to the Full $330 Billion Life-Sciences Services Universe

IQVIA’s total addressable market is the entire $330 billion life sciences service universe. This consists of three spending areas - CRO budgets, evidence and data, and commercialization.

IQVIA’s rivals compete inside one of these areas. CROs compete in trial execution, SaaS platforms compete in analytics and commercialization, and consulting firms compete in evidence and pricing strategies. IQVIA does it all. It just doesn’t sell a single service, but it sells the entire gamut from discovery to commercialization.

Historically, life sciences spending rotates across cycles; so when clinical dev is up, CROs make money, when commercialization becomes important, SaaS platforms benefit - but IQVIA makes money all the time. As management has noted, their new wins are increasingly coming at an earlier stage, continuing through evidence and launch - something that was rare a decade ago.

Valuation: Visibility, Cash and Buybacks

IQVIA’s valuation is based on cash-backed, repeatable EPS growth. Year to date, operating cash flow came at $1.919 billion, up from $1.831 billion in 2024. For just the quarter, OCF was $908 million, while capex stood at $136 million. Subtracting capex from OCF, I get FCF of $772 million - a record number for the company.

This year, the company had to pay an extra $37 million in interest expenses. Even then, they managed to improve their EPS, which went from $8.02 last year to $8.50 this year.

IQVIA is aggressively using its extra cash to retire shares. This year, so far, they spent over $1 billion on share buybacks, reducing share count by 10 million, or roughly 5-6%. Only cash flow, and no leverage, was used to fund this. So their balance sheet stayed in shape as far as debt was concerned.

Management guided for $16.15-$16.25 billion in revenue for FY25. Adjusted EBITDA and diluted EPS were to be $3.775-$3.8 billion and $11.85–$11.95 respectively. So what we have here is mid-single digit top-line growth, low-single digit EBITDA and high-single digit diluted EPS growth. Mostly buybacks, and some modest improvement in revenue mix, explains the EPS improvement this year.

IQVIA has a record $32.4 billion in backlog orders which support a 5% annual revenue growth. Their margin can be labeled stable to slightly improving. Margin is backed by Technology-assisted services, which is a high-margin segment seeing strong growth. They also have a strong buyback policy that contributes to a 3-4% EPS growth.

What that means in plain language is that the stock can go up even without a higher PE multiple. Earnings growth - in the high single to low double digit - will ensure that.

So there are three factors to properly valuing IQV. First is revenue conversion, which comes from the backlog. Second is the technology supported margin improvement, with a shift towards Technology & Analytics and AI-enabled services. Lastly, the extra cash driving buyback, which supports EPS growth without leverage.

So, there is no need for speculative rerating here. IQV simply needs to continue doing what it is already doing. That itself should be enough to see it grow ~10% annually.

Talking about price targets, if I take a base case of ~9% EPS growth into 2026 and a 22x forward multiple (a modest premium to today’s ~19x but still below faster-growing peers like Medpace (MEDP) at 37x), I arrive at a 12-month fair value of about $285 per share, within a reasonable range of $270–$300. At current prices, that is a 20-30% upside, not even accounting for the 5% FCF yield that goes into buybacks.

Why IQVIA’s Moat Is Competitive

IQVIA lists many competitors - Accenture, Panalgo, Optum Insight, Veeva, Oracle Health, ICON, Parexel, Syneos, Eversana and others. But IQVIA doesn’t have any single competitor across its entire product cycle. These peers can be divided across four broad types - CROs, SaaS/Analytics, Consulting firms and Big Tech. Each has its own sphere of operation, but not one of them overlaps into another.

This distinction makes IQVIA unique. When budget moves from clinical to evidence, CROs may suffer, but IQVIA stays fine because of its evidence capability. That's the same story for analytics or commercialization. That makes its business durable, unlike a company which operates in only a single budget cycle.

So a peer analysis isn’t being done to show who IQVIA is better than. Rather, it is to establish that no single competitor can easily replicate IQVIA’s model. So IQVIA’s compounding is insulated against competition.

Risks

There are a number of generic risks with IQVIA. Since their revenues depend on long-term contracts, any contract termination, even after termination penalties, hurts the business. The company also works with sensitive patient and other data, and new regulations, especially pertaining to data privacy, may create hurdles for the company.

IQVIA is a large company with global operations. That situation has inherent legal and FX complications. With the dollar going up or down, even an otherwise solid quarter may have negative FX repercussions.

IQVIA has a core technology platform, so software/hardware issues can cause disruptions. Finally, the company mentions in its risk discussion that consolidation among biopharma clients can cause pricing pressure.

None of these are specific risks, and do not change the company’s profile - nevertheless, they are to be monitored.

Bottom Line

IQVIA must not be viewed as just a clinical research organization. It has become the backbone of modern drug development - reason why most of the top 100 big pharma companies use its services.

IQVIA’s investment case rests on revenue visibility driven by backlog conversion, higher margin shift using technology, buyback-driven EPS growth, and high competitive hurdles stemming from its decades-long data assets.

data.title: JRI: Highest Premium To NAV Valuation In A Decade
data.description:
Overview

As market indices continue to trade near all-time highs and uncertainty remains around AI, it can be scary to accumulate equities at this time. Nuveen Real Asset Income and Growth Fund (JRI) offers investors a way to get exposure to 'real assets' on a global scale, while putting an emphasis on income generation. JRI puts a focus on preferred securities and a mix of debt investments, which is a way to offset the volatility of equities.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that JRI's share price has increased by about 2.1%. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 15.3% over the same time frame. JRI now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 11.7% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. While this makes JRI an enticing choice for income investors, I remain concerned about the overall sustainability of the payouts going forward. The fund's inconsistent performance means that the fund has consistently paid out more than it actually earns in income.

When I previously covered JRI, I issued a hold rating due to the suppressed valuation and heavy use of leverage. Since my last coverage, JRI has released an updated semi-annual report that prompted me to revisit the fund's performance and outlook as we approach the new year. As interest rates trend downward, I believe that we will see the fund's performance significantly improve as the underlying operating spread becomes more favorable. However, there are still some risks that investors should consider if maintaining a long-term position. For instance, the higher allocation to real estate securities presents a bit of concentration risk. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that JRI implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, JRI has total managed assets of $537.7M that are spread across a diverse range of assets. JRI has a listed expense ratio of 2.81% of total assets, but I think this is a little misleading because it includes fees related to interest and other operating costs. Excluding these other costs, the actual management fee sits at 0.95% of total assets. The fund's primary strategy is to allocate its assets to companies that are engaged in owning, operating, or developing infrastructure projects on a global scale. Additionally, the fund can allocate up to 40% of its assets into debt securities, which creates a balance between both growth potential and income generation. The fund's breakdown by asset type is as follows:

  • Real Estate Common: 25.1%.
  • Infrastructure Common: 24.3%.
  • Infrastructure Preferred: 19.3%.
  • Infrastructure Debt: 12.1%.
  • Real Estate Debt: 10%.
  • Real Estate Preferred: 8.6%.

Expanding on this diversity, JRI allocates its assets across a diverse range of sectors to mitigate concentration risks. As we can see below, JRI is most heavily allocated to Electric Utilities at 20.2% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Pipelines and Tech Infrastructure, which makes up 17.7% and 6.6% of assets respectively. The combination of the top three sectors makes up over 44% of total assets, so investors should anticipate that most of the daily price movement and total return will be influenced by these sectors.

Since the fund allocates its assets to real assets, JRI provides exposure to some of the highest quality companies in their respective industries. For instance, Evergy (EVRG) is the largest holding at 1.5% of assets. JRI also provides exposure to other notable companies such as Energy Transfer (ET), Enel Spa (ENLAY), National Grid (NGG), Gaming and Leisure Properties (GLPI), and Crown Castle (CCI), just to name a few.

Updated Valuation & Risk Profile

Since JRI is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the value of its underlying assets. As a result of the recent price increase, JRI now trades at a premium to NAV of 3.09%. However, the current premium price to NAV valuation makes the fund a bit too expensive when we consider the fact that JRI has traded at an average discount to NAV of 9.41% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that JRI now trades at one of the highest premium to NAV valuations over the last decade!

Since the time of my last coverage, JRI released an updated semi-annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.35 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.82 per share. Assuming a similar performance over the remainder of the 2025 period, JRI's net investment income is aligned to show some improvement over the last two years. This could be an indication that the underlying income generation within the fund is improving as interest rates continue to trend downward. However, net investment income isn't strong enough to support payouts on their own.

Therefore, JRI is reliant on positive net realized gains to bridge the gap in earnings. The challenge is that net realized gains are dependent on positive market momentum, which allows the fund to sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. Despite the real estate sector struggling to produce positive returns, the other areas of JRI's portfolio has offset this. Although the NAV growth has been minimal in recent years, the fund's NAV has remained resilient and avoided further losses. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $15.84 per share.
  • 2021: $17.41 per share.
  • 2022: $13.57 per share.
  • 2023: $13.69 per share.
  • 2024: $13.19 per share.
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $13.56 per share.

Despite the fund's diverse exposure across its portfolio, many of these real assets share a similar vulnerability to interest rates. Specifically, real estate and infrastructure related projects are typically reliant on access to cheap debt financing. Since interest rates remain elevated, the cost of debt remains expensive, and this may cause these businesses to delay projects and developments that can expand earnings.

Additionally, the fund utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings when possible. The use of leverage currently amounts to 31.79% of assets, which is equivalent to $170.9M. I consider this to be an aggressive amount of leverage in this higher interest rate environment. So not only are the holdings within the fund's portfolio impacted by higher rates, but so is JRI itself. Therefore, the operating spread between JRI's cost of leverage and the yield collected from its securities is thinner, which makes it more difficult to use leverage effectively. As long as interest rates remain elevated, the underlying earnings may remain suppressed.

Additionally, the fund allocates a sizeable portion of its assets to securities with poor credit ratings. For reference, borrowers with a credit rating below a BBB are classified as below investment grade and may present a greater risk of defaults. However, the fund still focuses a larger portion of its investments towards securities with stronger credit ratings. The full breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 46.8%.
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 49.4%.
  • Not Rated: 3.7%.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1335 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.7%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of approximately $1.602 per share. Despite the fund-raising its distribution over the last few years, JRI's underlying earnings are very inconsistent, and this makes me cautious about the sustainability of payouts. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: ($2.98) per share.
  • 2021: $2.73 per share.
  • 2022: ($2.68) per share.
  • 2023: $1.20 per share.
  • 2024: $0.93 per share.
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $1.17 per share.

As we can see, the last time JRI was able to out-earn the distribution was back in 2021. Assuming a similar performance over the remainder of 2025, there's a chance that JRI will be able to out-earn the distribution for the current year. Since earnings for 2025 are a bit stronger, I don't anticipate there will be any cuts in the near-term. However, the inconsistent performance means that the fund has historically paid out more than it actually earns. Therefore, I believe that the recent distribution raises were a bit strategic so that management can reduce the fund's price to NAV discount, rather than a move that indicates the fund's strength.

Additionally, investors should consider the fact that JRI's distributions offer little tax-efficiency. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that the distributions paid YTD were fueled by a combination of net investment income and return of capital. Therefore, a position in JRI would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize the tax impact of the income received.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain a hold rating on JRI at this time. Despite the variable earnings and distribution coverage, JRI has still been able to capture positive NAV growth over the last few years. However, the fund now trades at one of the highest premium to NAV valuations over the last decade, which is an indication that JRI is expensive at this time. However, I believe that management raised the dividend to reduce the fund's prior discount to NAV valuation. So we may need more time to assess the fund's performance throughout the first quarter of 2026 to see how JRI reacts to lower interest rates.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Disney Gets Mickey, Nemo, Hulk And C-3PO
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Along with a 3-year IP deal, Disney invests $1B in OpenAI. (0:15) Jobless claims rise. (2:22) End of an era for Macau. (3:04)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, Disney (DIS) is making a $1B equity investment in OpenAI (OPENAI) and has signed a sweeping three-year licensing deal that allows OpenAI’s video model Sora to generate content using Disney’s major intellectual properties.

Under the agreement, OpenAI can use more than 200 Disney, Marvel, Pixar, and Star Wars characters to create AI-generated video. Users will also be able to watch curated Sora-generated content on Disney+. The deal excludes talent likenesses and voices.

Disney will also become a major OpenAI customer — using its APIs to build new tools, products, and experiences, including for Disney+, and rolling out ChatGPT internally for employees.

Sora and ChatGPT Images are expected to begin generating fan-inspired videos featuring Disney’s licensed characters in early 2026.

OpenAI CEO Sam Altman called Disney “the global gold standard for storytelling,” saying the partnership expands how people create and experience content. Disney CEO Bob Iger said the deal marks a pivotal moment for the industry as the company integrates generative AI “thoughtfully and responsibly” while protecting creators.

The agreement arrives as Disney continues its legal fight with several AI startups accused of improperly using Disney and other studios’ characters.

Among active stocks, Visa (V) is higher after BofA upgraded the stock to Buy from Neutral, pointing to recent underperformance and standout fundamentals. Analyst Mihir Bhatia says Visa is one of only six S&P 500 companies delivering 10%+ revenue growth, double-digit EPS growth, and operating margins above 50% — and one of just two firms hitting those marks every year since 2021.

Adobe (ADBE) posted double-digit revenue growth in fiscal Q4 and sparked buzz with new AI initiatives and its pending Semrush (SEMR) acquisition. But some analysts echoed concerns that growth could slow in fiscal 2026 and want to see how much AI will reshape — or disrupt — Adobe’s creative-tools ecosystem.

And Eli Lilly (LLY) said its next-generation tongue twister weight-loss candidate retatrutide met all primary and key secondary endpoints in a late-stage study of obese or overweight adults with knee osteoarthritis but without diabetes.

Looking to the economy, weekly initial jobless claims jumped by +44K to 236K, above the 220K consensus and up sharply from 192K the prior week.

But continuing claims for the week ended Nov. 29 fell to 1.838M from 1.937M.

Pantheon Macro’s Samuel Tombs cautions that the drop in continuing claims is a head fake, noting the seasonal factor for Thanksgiving week is “unusually generous… for no clear reason.”

He adds that the rebound in initial claims — now at the highest level since early September after a similar adjustment-driven plunge last week — suggests layoffs may be starting to creep up, a signal economists are watching closely as labor-market momentum shows faint echoes of softening.

And in other news of note, Macau’s satellite casino era is coming to an end.

A 2022 amendment to Macau’s Gaming Law requires that all gaming operations take place in venues owned or directly controlled by the six licensed concessionaires — effectively outlawing the old satellite-casino model that relied on third-party property investors and revenue-sharing deals.

In response, major operators have accelerated the shutdown of nearly all remaining satellites, including Grand Dragon, Waldo, Grandview, and several Mocha Club slots venues. Some properties, like Ponte 16 and L’Arc Macau, have been acquired outright, so operators can bring them under full compliance.

The final holdout, Casino Landmark, is now scheduled to close on December 30, marking the end of the satellite system that once accounted for dozens of gaming locations across the city.

Macau’s six concessionaires — MGM China, Galaxy Entertainment, Venetian Macau, Melco Resorts & Entertainment, Wynn Resorts, and SJM Resorts — will now operate the entire legal gaming footprint under the new regime.

data.title: TBUX: A Worthy Ultra Short Option For Income
data.description:
Last week, I wrote an analysis for the Schwab Ultra-Short Income ETF (SCUS). I have been looking for worthy options to hold some cash as money market rates and yields on certificates of deposit continue their gradual descent. After completing my research on SCUS, I decided the fund was a Hold as its returns trailed several other peer ETFs. The Schwab fund also had a short track record, having opened in August of last year, which reduced my confidence in its future results.

With a little more research, I think I have landed on a better option, one that is worth buying for its income and low risk, the T. Rowe Price Ultra Short-Term Bond ETF (TBUX). This fund has a longer history, but it is not one of the better-known names in its category with less than $1 billion in assets under management. With a current yield above 4.7% and a portfolio of bonds that has an average credit rating of “A+”, investors looking to boost their fixed income returns may want to take a longer look at TBUX.

ETF Overview

The T. Rowe Price Ultra Short-Term Bond ETF has the stated objective of seeking a high level of income while maintaining low volatility of its principal value. According to T. Rowe Price, the fund should be able to consistently achieve a dividend payout that is above that of a money market fund, but of course, with greater gains come the added credit risk that also makes the fund slightly more volatile. The benchmark used for comparison to TBUX is the Bloomberg Short-Term Government/Corporate Index, but the fund is actively managed and may have results above or below the returns of that index.

TBUX has an inception date of September 28, 2021, so while it has a longer track record than SCUS, it is still a relatively new kid on the block compared to larger short-term bond ETFs. The fund has accumulated about $742.9 million in assets under management. TBUX is well diversified with just over 600 total holdings. The 10 largest investments in the fund are listed below.

Additional details about the holdings for the fund can be seen in the tables below. TBUX has its largest sector allocation to corporate debt with an investment of about 42.5% of the fund’s total assets. Securitized debt is right behind corporate bonds with a weight of 36.4%.

When looking at the rating of the bonds held by the fund, it is clear that TBUX drives much of its high level of income from walking out right to the edge of the investment grade scale. It’s highest bond allocation, from a rating perspective, is graded BBB and represents just over a third of the fund. However, the ETF balances that risk with an almost equally high allocation to AAA rated debt. This is how the fund achieves that “A+” average rating for its holdings.

The weighted average maturity for the TBUX portfolio is 1.38 years with a duration of just 0.67 years. The fund does have exposure to foreign markets as approximately a quarter of its assets are allocated to global bonds issued outside of the US. A few of those foreign investments could be seen in the top 10 holdings listed above.

ETF Analysis

During both the one- and three-year time periods, the T. Rowe Price Ultra Short-Term Bond ETF has been able to achieve total returns well ahead of its benchmark. The fund was started in 2021, just in time for the bond market to have one of its worst years on record in 2022. Nonetheless, when looking at the comparison between TBUX and its comparison index, the differences are significant, with TBUX exceeding the returns of its benchmark by 1% on an annual basis since 2022.

However, the benchmark used by T. Rowe Price does not seem to be the most accurate reflection of the fund’s holdings. The Bloomberg Short-Term Government/Corporate Index is much more heavily weighted toward US Treasury Bonds and Notes. At the end of November, TBUX had 55.4% of its portfolio allocated to corporate bonds and notes. Its stated benchmark only allocated 22.8% of its assets in that sector. This helps explain the higher returns for TBUX as the fund is taking on the added risk of slightly lower rated debt. In 2022, this added risk did not pay off for the T Rowe Price ETF as it declined 0.12% that year while its benchmark was able to squeeze out a 0.69% gain as shown in the calendar year performance chart below.

The dividend yield for TBUX is 4.79% over the last 12 months. Payouts from the fund are made on a monthly basis. The final dividend payment of the year, which is generally made in the final week of December, is traditionally the largest for the ETF. Last year’s final payout was $0.30, 27.1% above the next closest in size for 2024. In 2023, the final dividend payment was $0.295, 34.8% ahead of its closest competitor. It is likely that this year’s December payment, while still larger than the others from 2025, will be smaller than those from the last two years. The fund has seen its yield decline gradually over the last 18 months as short-term rates have declined.

Comparison to Other Ultra-Short ETF Options

For a peer comparison, I used two funds that I referenced in my previous analysis of SCUS. All of the ETFs listed below are taxable bond funds in the ultra-short sub classification. They all have similar stated objectives and hold a mix of corporate, securitized, and government debt. Each one is actively managed.

  • Schwab Ultra-Short Income ETF (SCUS)
  • JPMorgan Ultra-Short Income ETF (JPST)
  • Vanguard Ultra-Short Bond ETF Shares (VUSB)

The results from my comparison are given in the chart below. As can be seen, TBUX tops all three of these peer funds in total returns for each time period listed. (SCUS does not yet have a three-year history because it was started in 2024.) TBUX also tops each of the funds with the highest dividend yield.
