Risks to Consider

The T. Rowe Price Ultra Short-Term Bond ETF is actively managed and therefore there is always the chance that its performance can fall short of its benchmark as well as its peer ETFs. While the fund has a relatively short lifespan, it has been managed by the same person since its inception. The manager has two decades of experience with T Rowe Price and 17 years of investment experience. This alleviates some, but not all of the potential concerns for the fund’s performance given its shorter history.

Even as the Federal Reserve voted to lower short-term interest rates on December 10, there is some uncertainty about what will happen with rates next year. The bonds held by TBUX will fall in value if interest rates start to move higher in the future. Because this fund holds both domestic and international bonds, some of its holdings are also subject to interest rate risks in other countries. Those foreign bonds would also carry added risk of currency rate fluctuations, political turmoil, and other economic events outside of the US.

The fund has traded at a premium to its net asset value for an extended period of time. During 2024, there was only one day that TBUX traded at a discount to its NAV. In 2025, it has only had three days in that range. The trend in the third quarter of this year was moving closer to the fund’s true value, but there have been multiple occasions in the last 18 months when the fund was trading 10 basis points or more above its NAV. The differences in value may impact the fund’s results and may also change some of the rankings for the fund when compared to its peers. A graph that shows the history of the fund’s premium or discount over the last two years is shown below.

The Bottom Line

TBUX is rated as a Sell by Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system. However, the only low score assigned to the ETF is a “D-” in momentum. When the returns of a bond fund are compared to the performance for all ETFs, including equity funds, they rarely look attractive during periods of growth in the overall market. There are 591 taxable bond ETFs currently scored by Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating. None of them are currently rated as a Buy, and only 10 earn even a Hold rating. Take away the momentum score, and I think the rest of this quant analysis matches up with my own.

I consider the T. Rowe Price Ultra Short-Term Bond ETF to be a Buy for investors who are looking for income that exceeds current CD and money market rates. The risk for a decline in NAV is certainly present, but it is minimized by the high quality of the bonds held by the fund and their short duration.

data.title: United States Lime & Minerals: Great Company, But No Margin Of Safety
data.description:
Investment Thesis

United States Lime & Minerals, Inc. (USLM) is a lime- and limestone-producing company. For the last 5 years, it has shown improvements in revenue growth. Revenues rose at a CAGR of 14.6% from 2020 to 2024. Cost of revenue margins decreased from 78% in 2015 to 55% in 2024. Operating margins increased from 21% in 2020 to 39% in 2024. The business has no debt, and most of its assets are allocated in current assets, of which 79% is cash.

Despite the cyclicality of the industries USLM serves, the company’s financial history shows resilience during market downturns. During the Great Financial Crisis, from 2008 to 2009, revenues dropped 12.5% and then recovered 13.4% from 2009 to 2010. Operating margins improved during that time (16.4%, 17.9%, and 20.9% in 2008, 2009, and 2010). In the COVID-19 pandemic, revenues increased by 1.5% from 2019 to 2020, and operating margins went from 18.5% to 21.1%; margins boomed in 2021 to 37%.

Thanks to the increase in revenues, operating efficiencies, a great balance sheet, and solid fundamentals, the stock soared ~1,160.5%. It went from a low of $12.60 (split-adjusted) in March 2020 to an all-time high price of $160 (split-adjusted) in November 2024. The stock then corrected 48.65% at the beginning of the year, then recovered 61.98%. It went from a low of $82.98 in March to $134.43 in October 2025.

However, the current valuations of this business don’t provide a margin of safety at the current price of $119 per share (as of December 3rd, 2025), and caution for long-term investors is recommended. For example, Microsoft in the 2000s was a great business, but it was very overvalued. As value investors, we don’t want to overpay for assets, even if they are great. Instead, we buy great companies at low or fair prices.

An in-depth analysis reveals the market is pricing in very high growth, which might not materialize when considering USLM’s core market, its positioning in the industry, and the company’s history. At a current P/E multiple of ~27x and a stock price of $119 (as of December 3rd, 2025), the company is overvalued.

Company Overview

USLM is a lime and limestone producer and seller that was founded in Dallas, Texas, in 1945. The company went public in 1995 and was listed on the NASDAQ Composite Index. It produces and sells pulverized limestone, or PLS, aggregate, hydrated lime, and lime slurry from its high-quality limestone open-pit quarries and underground mines.

USLM mines and produces crushed limestone, which is sold as aggregate material for construction. PLS is used in construction to produce roof shingles, asphalt paving, and the production of glass. It’s also used as an additive to agricultural feeds and soil enhancement. It’s used for flue gas treatment for desulfurization systems in coal-fired power plants, as well as other industries that require scrubbing of emissions for environmental and mine safety purposes (2024 10-K).

Quicklime is used in the metal industry to process steel. It’s used for soil stabilization in highways, roads, building construction, oilfield roads, and drill sites. It’s used in municipal sanitation, water treatment facilities, and to manufacture paper products. Hydrated lime is used in construction, flue gas and water treatment, in asphalt as an anti-stripping agent, in the production of chemicals, and as a conditioning agent for oil and gas drilling mud. Lime slurry is mainly used in construction.

Mine/Locations

  • Texas Lime Quarry (Texas)
  • Batesville Quarry (Arkansas)
  • Love Hollow Quarry (Arkansas)
  • St. Clair Mine (Oklahoma)
  • Carthage Mine (Missouri)
  • Mill Creek Quarry (Oklahoma)
  • Monarch Pass Quarry (Colorado)

USLM can reach customers within a radius of ~400 miles of each of the company’s plants.

USLM has a great position in the south-centric regions of the country. This has allowed it to maintain its customer base, endure, and thrive in its highly competitive, concentrated, and high-barrier-to-entry market. This is thanks to the scarcity of high-quality limestone deposits, for which zoning and permitting are required for the extraction of the stone materials that can be mined, produced, and sold (F2023 10-K).

The company highlights the need for lime plants and facilities located close to markets and relevant industries to reinforce the premium value of operations. This includes having permitted, long-term, high-quality limestone reserves with great locations and transportation, where demand is high and sustainable.

ULSM possesses a narrow moat, thanks to its strategic locations, low-cost production, and the high barriers to entry. However, we’ll see shortly that despite being a great company, the current valuation doesn’t provide a margin of safety for long-term investors.

Valuation Analysis

Following Bruce Greenwald's valuation methodology, I will present a Net Asset Value model (NAV) and an Earnings Power Value model (EPV).

Net Asset Value

To determine the NAV, I calculated the reproduction cost of its assets. The most difficult assets to value were USLM’s mineral reserves and land. Most of the information about the costs incurred to purchase them is not reported in the 10-Ks. I undertook the endeavor of estimating purchase prices based on past transactions disclosed by USLM and current listing prices for quarries in the US.

For mineral reserves and land, I calculated a 1.85x multiple to the 2024 book value. For proven natural gas properties, I applied a 1.5x multiple. For buildings, leasehold improvements, machinery and equipment, furniture and fixtures, and automotive equipment, I applied a ~0.85x multiple to account for depreciation and inflation.

All $ in millions

Intangibles are not accounted for in the balance sheet. But they must be accounted for.

Current liabilities remain the same as the latest current book value (Q3 2025), and the only adjustment is for deferred tax liabilities, which are valued at half of book value.

The NAV on an equity basis is $933.6 million. Adding back $0 of total debt and subtracting cash and non-core assets gives us an NAV on an enterprise basis of $583.7 million.

Earnings Power Value

USLM revenues increased rapidly from 2021 to 2025 because of the rapid increase in prices for lime and limestone products. The company has a stable customer base and volume of sales, which limits the revenue growth. Fortunately, this commodity is very stable, and prices increase rapidly during economic booms and remain stable during economic busts.

The correlation is evident. This allows us to estimate sustainable revenues.

A conservative estimate of sustainable revenues is $300 million. A sustainable operating margin is ~33%, and 19% should be a sustainable corporate tax rate. After adjusting to account for the cost of replacement capex, I computed a sustainable earnings power of $76.9 million. Let’s compute the cost of capital.

USLM has no debt. Thus, the cost of equity is the cost of capital. I believe USLM is at the low end of the normal-risk cyclical equities, but it also belongs to the high end of quality, low-leverage businesses. Therefore, 8% is an appropriate estimate of USLM’s cost of capital. The earnings power divided by the cost of capital yields an EPV of $961.2 million (EV basis) and $1,311.2 million on an equity basis.

Let’s compare NAV & EPV on both bases.

Case C company - NAV < EPV

This valuation yields an intrinsic value on an equity basis of $1,122.4 million, or $39 per share. But this is a Case C company (franchise businesses with an economic moat), therefore, the value of growth takes a greater weight on USLM’s valuation, rendering the NAV & EPV calculations pretty much useless, as they only point us to value the growth instead.

Franchise Value Model (For Case C Companies)

The models help us compute a benchmark return, which assumes that the current market cap and the intrinsic value are equal, and what the return an investor would get today if they bought equity from USLM.

The three components are cash return (net dividends and buybacks), the organic growth rate (growth rate of USLM’s core market), and the active reinvestment return. The sum of these rates, minus the fade rate, computes the benchmark return, which is then compared to the cost of capital to see if there is a margin of safety.

The current cash return for investors is 0.2%. The US lime and limestone market is expected to have a CAGR of 3.5% (in tandem with the average growth rate of the US GDP). The active reinvestment return is 4%, and the fade rate is about 0.9%. This gives us a benchmark growth return at the current market price of 6.8%. Compared to the cost of capital of 8%, we see that it’s lower. Therefore, there is no margin of safety.

Market Expectations Analysis

While we have thoroughly valued this company, I like to assess market expectations with a reverse DCF model.

Assuming the company maintains a 33% operating margin, COGS margins of 60.5%, a corporate tax rate of 19%, a cost of capital of 8%, and working capital trails revenue growth for the next 30 years, the market expects a ~5.5% revenue CAGR at a current price of $119 and $120.

Experts in the industry and historical trends indicate that the US lime and limestone market is expected to grow 3.5% in the long term, so how can USLM be expected to grow faster than the expected CAGR of its industry over the next 30 years? The scenario is unlikely. If that were to be the case, USLM would become the US lime and limestone market itself.

If we use the same model to yield a 3.5% CAGR, the estimated intrinsic value of equity is $2,721.2 million, or $95.3 per share. The current price would be overpriced by ~26%. This also means that if a 3.5% CAGR materializes, the market is overestimating revenue growth by ~57%.

Risks

Lime and limestone prices could keep rising rapidly for many years, since this is normal for this commodity. This could help USLM’s revenues to rise further than expected, and thanks to its strategic investments to improve operations, margins and earnings will be higher, helping the stock price to rise further. I could also be underestimating the future CAGR for the US lime and limestone industry, which could grow at a faster rate than previously expected. Maybe it does grow 5% per year, or even 6% per year, which would warrant a higher growth rate of return for USLM. Also, USLM could grow faster than its industry for some time, allowing the company to gain more market share (through more acquisitions and expansion). Another risk is that the benchmark growth return could be a lot higher; I could be underestimating either the organic growth rate or the active reinvestment return.

Recommendation

USLM is clearly a great company, and the recent increase in revenues piqued the interest of Wall Street and investors alike. Thanks to the improvement in revenues, operating efficiencies, operating margins, no debt, solid balance sheet, conservative management, modest competitive advantages (thanks to the geographical position of its limestone quarries and low-cost production), and its sporadic but successful investment strategy, the stock rose rapidly, providing fantastic returns for investors. However, an intelligent investor knows that no matter how great a company is, we shouldn’t pay great prices for it. Instead, for companies that consistently grow and provide value, we want to pay fair prices. How we determine those fair prices depends a lot on the valuation methods we use and the assumptions we make about growth.

I provide a different approach by calculating a benchmark return that can be compared to the cost of capital to determine a margin of safety. At the current market cap of $3.4 billion, this benchmark return is approximately 6.8%, which is less than the cost of capital of 8%. This offers no margin of safety for long-term investors, and a lower price is required to make USLM an attractive investment. All things equal, at a market cap of $1.5 billion, investors would get a 12.2% benchmark return for their investment in USLM, which would provide a margin of safety of 34.3%. For investors to get an equal benchmark return to the cost of capital, the market cap must be ~$2.65 billion.

On the other hand, if we take the riskier approach by trying to determine USLM’s revenue CAGR for the next 30 years and assume it will achieve a 3.5% growth rate per annum, the intrinsic value of equity would be $2.7 billion. Investors would need the market cap to drop to $1.8 billion to get a decent margin of safety.

For all these reasons, I give USLM an underweight (Sell) rating, with a fair value per share of $93–$95 per share.

data.title: Nikkei 225 Slips As SoftBank Slump And BOJ Hike Fear Pressure Tech Stocks
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

The Nikkei 225 fell 0.9% to close near 50,149 on Thursday as a sharp slide in SoftBank (SFTBY) erased early-session gains and dragged Japan’s heavyweight tech names lower. Oracle’s (ORCL) steep post-earnings decline spilled into Asian markets, triggering a 7% drop in SoftBank and weakening sentiment across semiconductor equipment makers, industrials and major consumer electronics stocks.

The session began on steady footing, but momentum cracked mid-day as SoftBank’s slide cascaded across the index. Lasertec (LSRCY), Fujikura (FKURF), Mitsubishi Heavy (MHVYF), Sanrio (SNROF) and Nintendo (NTDOY) all turned lower, reflecting a broad shift to risk-off positioning. Investors grew cautious not only because of the global tech earnings reset but also due to the possibility that the BOJ could introduce its first rate hike of the cycle. That combination left buyers reluctant to defend early advances.

Technical picture shows fatigue, but broader uptrend intact

From a structural standpoint, the Nikkei remains in an established uptrend, though recent price action shows signs of cooling. The index sits directly above the 20-day EMA at 50,061, a level that has served as a stabilizing pivot throughout the past month. The 50-day EMA at 48,871 holds further below, anchoring the medium-term trend.

However, repeated failures to push beyond the 51,500 region highlight fading momentum. RSI at 52 reflects neutrality rather than the high-energy thrust seen during the October breakout. Price ranges have tightened over the past two weeks, a classic sign that market participants are waiting for a clearer macro signal before committing to fresh positions.

Short-term structure is more fragile. On the 30-minute chart, the Supertrend flipped bearish after SoftBank’s slide triggered a sharp intraday decline. SAR dots remain above price, reinforcing downward pressure. An initial attempt to rebound from the 50,000 level stalled at resistance near the Supertrend line at 50,544. Until the index can reclaim that intraday pivot, short-term risks point toward retests of 49,900 and 49,700. These levels acted as key accumulation zones last week and will determine whether dip-buyers remain engaged.

Policy risks and global tech sentiment shape near-term direction

Despite the pullback, the broader backdrop for Japanese equities remains constructive. Corporate governance reforms, strong capital spending and steady foreign inflows have been reliable supports throughout the year. The long-term trend remains intact as long as the Nikkei holds above the 50-day EMA.

The immediate risk lies in policy divergence. A BOJ rate hike next week would widen the gap between Japanese tightening and global easing expectations. A firmer yen would pressure export-heavy and yen-sensitive sectors, which have powered the index’s run to historic highs. If the currency strengthens sharply, the Nikkei could find it harder to maintain its upper range.

Global tech sentiment also remains a swing factor. Oracle’s earnings-driven drop has shaken confidence in a sector that heavily influences the index. If U.S. megacaps remain volatile into next week’s data releases, Japanese tech could remain under pressure.

For now, the Nikkei stands at an inflection point. Holding above 50,000 would allow consolidation and another attempt at 51,500. Losing that shelf could unwind momentum toward the 48,800–49,000 region where the 50-day EMA and earlier breakout structure converge.

Outlook as traders watch BOJ decision

Market attention now shifts to whether today’s weakness was a SoftBank-specific shock or an early signal of broader hesitation ahead of next week’s BOJ meeting. The policy outcome, combined with global earnings sentiment and yen behavior, will determine whether the index resumes its upward trajectory or slips into a deeper consolidation phase.

Previously, we discussed how export strength, foreign inflows and improved governance kept Japan’s market resilient even during global tech volatility. The current pullback echoes that theme: the uptrend remains intact, but short-term direction hinges on whether tightening risks materialize.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.
